title: Model Serving: Reaching the World
concept: Model serving is the process of making a loaded, trained model accessible to other software systems, so apps and websites can actually use its predictions.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does model versioning let a team do?

options.text: Track distinct model files, allowing comparison and instant rollback if a new version underperforms.
Automatically merge all trained models into one file.

exercises.explain: Correct! Versioning enables comparison and safe rollback between model versions.

cards.title: The chef with no waiters
cards.body: Imagine a brilliant chef cooking incredible meals in a kitchen with no doors, windows, or waiters. No matter how good the food is, nobody outside the kitchen can ever taste it. A trained model sitting alone on a laptop is exactly like that chef — brilliant, but completely useless to anyone else.

cards.title: Model Serving
cards.body: Model serving is the practice of making a loaded model accessible to other systems — like a mobile app, website, or another server — so they can send it new data and receive predictions back.

cards.title: Opening a restaurant window
cards.body: Serving a model is like installing a takeout window on that hidden kitchen. Now customers (other apps) can walk up, place an order (send input data), and receive their meal (a prediction) — without ever needing to enter the kitchen themselves.

cards.title: A mobile app calling a model
cards.body: A fitness app wants to estimate a user's calorie burn. Instead of running the model directly on the phone, the app sends the user's workout data to a server where the model is being served. The server runs the loaded model and sends back a calorie estimate instantly.

cards.title: Serving isn't the same as training
cards.body: You might think 'serving' just means the model is sitting on a server somewhere. In reality, serving specifically means the model is actively set up to receive requests and respond with predictions in real time — a passive, unused file on a server isn't being 'served' at all.

cards.title: Unserved vs. Served Model
cards.body: The difference between a trained model and a usable one:

left.text:
Sits as a file on one laptop.
No other system can access it.
Effectively useless to others.

right.text:
Actively listens for requests.
Any authorized app can call it.
Returns predictions on demand.

options.text: Making a loaded model accessible to other systems so they can request predictions.
Training a model for the very first time.
Cleaning missing values from a dataset.

exercises.explain: Correct! Serving makes a trained model actively accessible to other systems.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A trained model file that just sits unused on a developer's personal laptop is considered 'served'.
exercises.explain: False! A model must be actively accessible to other systems to be considered served, not just saved somewhere.

exercises.sentence: Making a trained model accessible to other apps and systems is called model ___.
exercises.explain: Serving is what makes a model useful beyond just the developer's own machine.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario describes model serving in action?

options.text: A weather app sends today's readings to a server, which runs a loaded model and returns a rain probability.
A developer trains a model and closes their laptop without saving or sharing it.
A team manually calculates predictions using a calculator instead of any model.

exercises.explain: Correct! A server actively responding to a request with a prediction is model serving in action.

exercises.prompt: A data scientist builds an excellent model on her laptop that predicts customer churn with 95% accuracy, but the company's website has no way to send it new customer data or receive predictions back. What is missing, and why does it matter?

options.text: The model isn't being served — without exposing it to other systems, the website can never actually use its predictions, no matter how accurate it is.
Nothing is missing — a 95% accurate model is automatically usable by any system.
The model needs to be retrained with more data before anything else can happen.
The laptop needs a faster processor.

exercises.explain: Exactly! No matter how accurate a model is, it's useless to other systems until it's actually served. Next, let's look at the specific technical mechanism used to make this connection: APIs!
